questions.question: Criminal attempt is a common law inchoate offence. What are the two essential elements of criminal attempt?

options.A: The accused must have planned to commit an offence and must have taken any step, however remote, in the direction of that offence
options.B: The accused must have: (1) the full mens rea for the completed offence (the specific intention to commit the offence); and (2) performed an act that goes beyond mere preparation and is immediately connected or proximate to the completed offence — the 'proximity test'
options.C: The accused must have taken an act in furtherance of a plan to commit an offence; recklessness as to whether the offence would be committed is sufficient mens rea for attempt
options.D: The accused must have performed the actus reus of the completed offence but failed to complete it due to external circumstances

questions.explanation: Criminal attempt has two essential components: (1) Mens rea — the accused must have the full specific intention to commit the completed offence; recklessness or negligence is insufficient for attempt; if the completed offence requires intention to kill, the attempt requires intention to kill (not merely recklessness as to death); and (2) Actus reus — the accused must have performed an act that goes beyond mere preparation; this 'proximity test' requires that the act be more than merely preparatory and be immediately connected to the commission of the completed offence. The line between preparation (not criminal) and attempt (criminal) is one of the central questions in attempt law. Option A incorrectly accepts any step however remote (preparation would be sufficient). Option C incorrectly accepts recklessness as sufficient mens rea. Option D incorrectly requires actual performance of the actus reus of the completed offence — many attempts are stopped before the actus reus is complete.

questions.question: In AG v Thornton [1952] (Ireland), the court considered the requirements for criminal attempt. What did the case establish regarding the Irish approach to attempt?

options.A: The case established that any act done with criminal intent constitutes an attempt — the specific intent and even a single preparatory step is sufficient
options.B: The case confirmed that attempt in Irish law requires: (1) specific intent to commit the completed offence; and (2) an act that is proximate and immediately connected to the offence — mere preparation, even if done with criminal intent, is insufficient; the act must be part of the commission of the offence itself
options.C: The case established that Irish law does not recognise the proximity test — any overt act in furtherance of a criminal intent constitutes attempt
options.D: The case held that attempt requires proof that the accused would have succeeded in completing the offence but for external intervention

questions.explanation: AG v Thornton [1952] is the leading Irish authority on criminal attempt. The case confirmed that Irish law applies the proximity test: there must be specific intent to commit the completed offence, and the act must go beyond mere preparation to be proximate and immediately connected to the commission of the offence. Mere preparation, even with criminal intent, is not attempt. The court drew the crucial distinction between acts of preparation (which are not criminal attempt) and acts that are part of the commission of the offence itself (which are). This distinction is not always easy to apply — it is a matter of degree based on the specific facts of each case. Thornton established that Irish law takes a relatively strict approach to the proximity test, requiring the act to be immediately connected to the completed offence. Option A incorrectly accepts preparation as sufficient. Option C incorrectly suggests Irish law rejects the proximity test. Option D incorrectly requires proof of likely success.

questions.question: In R v Campbell [1991] CA (England and Wales), the accused was found outside a post office with a fake gun and a threatening note, intending to commit robbery. He was arrested before entering. The court considered whether his conduct constituted attempted robbery. What did the court hold?

options.A: The court held that Campbell was guilty of attempted robbery — his proximity to the post office and possession of the items demonstrated that he had gone beyond preparation
options.B: The court held that Campbell had not gone beyond preparation — being outside the post office with the implements of the planned robbery did not cross the threshold into attempt; further steps (such as entering the post office) were required to constitute attempt
options.C: The court held that Campbell was guilty of attempted robbery on the basis that he had taken all steps within his control and only external circumstances prevented completion
options.D: The court held that possession of the fake gun and threatening note constituted a separate attempt offence distinct from attempted robbery

questions.explanation: R v Campbell [1991] CA is a significant case on the difficult line between preparation and attempt. The Court of Appeal held that Campbell's presence outside the post office with a fake gun and threatening note did not go beyond preparation — it did not constitute attempted robbery. Despite his clear intent and physical proximity to the target, Campbell had not yet taken the step that crossed the threshold into attempt (he had not entered the post office, had not approached any victim, and had not made any threatening demands). The case illustrates how demanding the proximity test can be: even circumstances that strongly suggest criminal intent may remain on the preparation side of the line. For an attempt, the act must be immediately and directly connected to the commission of the offence. Option A incorrectly treats proximity and possession of implements as sufficient. Option C applies an 'all steps within control' test that is not the law. Option D creates a non-existent separate offence.

questions.question: In AG v Richmond [1935] (Ireland), the accused was found near a hay rick with combustible materials on a dry, windy night. He was charged with attempted arson. The court held this constituted attempt. What principle does this case illustrate?

options.A: The case illustrates that preparation can constitute attempt when it involves specialist or dangerous materials — possession of combustible materials is itself an attempt
options.B: The case illustrates that where the accused is at the scene of the intended offence, in possession of the materials necessary to carry it out, and appears to be in the process of embarking on the commission of the offence, the proximity test may be satisfied — the totality of the circumstances (location, materials, conditions) can cross the threshold from preparation into attempt
options.C: The case illustrates that arson is a special category of offence where the proximity test is applied more leniently than for other offences
options.D: The case illustrates that Irish courts apply the same test for attempt as for conspiracy — any step in furtherance of a criminal design constitutes an attempt

questions.explanation: AG v Richmond [1935] is an important Irish case illustrating where the preparation/attempt line falls in practice. The court held that the accused's presence near the hay rick with combustible materials on conditions suitable for arson (dry, windy night) crossed the threshold into attempt. Unlike R v Campbell (where the accused was outside a post office with implements but had not yet begun to act), Richmond was apparently in the process of embarking on the very act of starting the fire — the circumstances suggested the act of arson was imminent and the accused was in the execution phase rather than the preparatory phase. Richmond illustrates that the proximity test is fact-sensitive and that courts will look at the totality of circumstances: where is the accused? What do they have? What are they doing? The contrast between Richmond (attempt) and Campbell (preparation only) illustrates the difficulty in applying the proximity test. Option A incorrectly creates a materials-based test. Option C incorrectly suggests arson has a more lenient test. Option D conflates the attempt and conspiracy standards.

questions.question: In People (AG) v O'Sullivan [1930] (Ireland), the court articulated the 'unequivocal act' formulation of the proximity test for attempt. What does this test require?

options.A: The accused's act must be so unambiguous that only one reasonable interpretation is possible — that the accused was in the process of committing the offence; an equivocal act that could be innocent or criminal does not satisfy the test
options.B: The 'unequivocal act' test requires that the accused have made a public declaration of intent before taking any act — unequivocal refers to the communication of intent, not the nature of the act
options.C: The 'unequivocal act' formulation requires that the accused have committed the complete actus reus of the offence before attempt can be established
options.D: The test requires that no other conclusion is possible other than that the accused intended to commit the specific offence charged — ambiguity as to which of several possible offences was intended prevents conviction for attempt

questions.explanation: People (AG) v O'Sullivan [1930] articulated the 'unequivocal act' test as one formulation of the proximity test for attempt. Under this test, the act committed by the accused must be so unambiguous in its nature that it points clearly to an attempt to commit the specific offence — an act that is equivocal (capable of innocent or criminal interpretation) may not satisfy the test. The idea is that an attempted crime is distinguishable from innocent preparation by its unequivocal character: a person reaching into someone's pocket is unequivocally attempting theft in the context of the other circumstances; a person walking down the street is not. The unequivocal act test has been criticised for being too demanding — some very clear attempts may involve individually equivocal acts. Nevertheless, it remains an important Irish formulation. Option B mischaracterises 'unequivocal' as relating to public communication. Option C requires the completed actus reus which goes beyond attempt. Option D focuses on ambiguity regarding which offence was intended rather than the nature of the act.

questions.question: In R v White [1910] CA (England and Wales), the accused put a small quantity of poison (insufficient to kill) into his mother's drink with the intention of killing her. His mother died of an unrelated heart attack before consuming enough poison to die from it. The court held this constituted attempted murder. What principle does this establish?

options.A: The case establishes that attempted murder requires proof that the victim was actually endangered — the accused's act must have been objectively capable of causing death
options.B: The case establishes that impossibility is not a defence to attempt where the accused acts with the requisite intent — the fact that the accused's act was incapable (due to the insufficient quantity of poison) of achieving the intended result does not prevent conviction for attempt; what matters is the intent and the performance of the proximate act
options.C: The case establishes that where the victim dies (even from an unrelated cause) during an attempt, the accused is guilty of murder rather than attempted murder
options.D: The case establishes that using an insufficient quantity of a lethal substance is mere preparation and cannot constitute attempted murder

questions.explanation: R v White [1910] CA is a classic authority on attempt and impossibility. The court held that White was guilty of attempted murder despite the fact that the quantity of poison he used was insufficient to kill. The case establishes that an attempt is not defeated because the accused's chosen method was objectively incapable of achieving the intended result — what matters is: (1) the accused had the full intent to commit the completed offence (intent to kill); and (2) the accused performed an act proximate to that offence (administering poison). The insufficiency of the poison went to whether the murder could succeed, not to whether the attempt was committed. Note also that the mother's death from an independent cause (heart attack) did not make White guilty of murder — there was no causal link between the poisoning and the death, so the conviction remained for attempted murder only. Option A incorrectly requires that the act be objectively capable of causing death. Option C incorrectly holds that any death during an attempt elevates the charge to murder. Option D incorrectly characterises administering poison as mere preparation.

questions.question: In R v Pearman [1985] CA (England and Wales), the court considered how intention is established in attempt cases. What principle did the court confirm?

options.A: The court held that for attempt, the accused must give a formal statement of intent before taking any action — implied intent inferred from acts is insufficient
options.B: The court confirmed that the trier of fact (jury) may draw inferences of intent from the acts performed by the accused — the specific intent required for attempt can be established circumstantially from the nature and circumstances of the accused's acts, without requiring a direct confession or statement of intent
options.C: The court held that the intent for attempt must be established beyond a reasonable doubt by direct evidence — circumstantial inference of intent is insufficient for conviction
options.D: The court held that recklessness as to the consequences of the act is sufficient to establish the mens rea for most forms of attempt

questions.explanation: R v Pearman [1985] CA confirmed the important principle that intention in attempt cases may be established by inference from the accused's conduct. The jury is entitled to look at what the accused did and draw reasonable inferences about what they intended. This is consistent with general principles of mens rea — intent is rarely established by direct confession and is typically inferred from all the surrounding circumstances. For attempt specifically, this means that the combination of the act performed, the circumstances, the target of the act, and any other evidence can support an inference that the accused specifically intended to commit the completed offence. Pearman prevents the accused from arguing that absence of a direct statement of intent defeats an attempt charge. Options A and C incorrectly require direct evidence of intent. Option D incorrectly introduces recklessness as sufficient mens rea for attempt.

questions.question: In DPP v Douglas and Hayes [1985] ILRM (Ireland), the court made a critically important ruling on the mens rea for attempted murder. What did the court hold?

options.A: The court held that attempted murder requires only an intention to cause serious harm — if the accused intended grievous bodily harm and the victim survives, this constitutes attempted murder
options.B: The court held that attempted murder requires an intention to KILL — it is not sufficient that the accused intended to cause serious bodily harm; the full mens rea for attempted murder is the specific intention to cause the death of another person; an intention to cause serious but non-lethal harm is insufficient
options.C: The court held that attempted murder and completed murder have the same mens rea — in Ireland, both can be established by recklessness as to death
options.D: The court held that attempted murder requires proof of both intention to kill and planning — spontaneous attempts do not satisfy the mens rea requirement

questions.explanation: DPP v Douglas and Hayes [1985] ILRM is an exam-critical case on attempted murder in Irish law. The court held that attempted murder requires the specific intention to KILL — not merely an intention to cause serious bodily harm. This is a significant difference from completed murder, which in Ireland can be established by an intention to cause serious bodily harm (under the Conroy rule from the SCC in DPP v Murray [1977]). For attempted murder, however, the accused must have intended to bring about death specifically. An accused who intended to cause serious but not fatal injury and the victim survived has not committed attempted murder — they may be guilty of assault causing serious harm, but not attempted murder. This asymmetry between the mens rea for murder (intent to kill OR intent to cause serious harm) and attempted murder (intent to kill only) is a frequent examination topic. The maximum sentence for attempted murder in Ireland is 20 years. Option A incorrectly accepts intent to cause serious harm. Options C and D misstate the requirements.

questions.question: Colin picks up an empty wallet that he believes contains money. He is arrested as he is taking it. Can he be convicted of attempted theft?

options.A: No — the wallet is empty, making theft impossible; impossibility is a complete defence to attempt where the completed offence could never have been committed
options.B: Yes — Colin had the full intention to steal (he believed the wallet contained money) and performed an act proximate to theft (picking up the wallet); the fact that it happened to be empty (making the theft technically impossible) does not prevent conviction for attempt; impossibility due to factual circumstances is generally not a defence to attempt
options.C: No — theft requires the taking of property that actually belongs to another; since the wallet is empty, there is no property to steal and no offence is committed
options.D: Yes — but the conviction would be for handling rather than theft, since Colin is taking an item without knowing its contents

questions.explanation: Colin can be convicted of attempted theft. The general principle in Irish (and English) law is that factual impossibility is not a defence to attempt. Colin had: (1) the full mens rea for theft — he believed the wallet contained money and intended to steal it (dishonest intent to permanently deprive); and (2) the actus reus of attempt — he picked up the wallet, which is a proximate act immediately connected to the theft. The fact that the wallet was empty is a factual circumstance that made the completed offence impossible in this instance — but impossibility due to factual circumstances (the wallet happened to be empty, rather than the concept of 'stealing from a wallet' being legally impossible) does not afford a defence to attempt. Compare with DPP v Nock [1978] HOL on conspiracy, where impossibility did operate as a defence — the position for attempt is generally more favourable to the prosecution. Option A incorrectly applies impossibility as a complete defence to attempt. Option C incorrectly focuses on whether there was property to steal rather than the accused's belief and intent. Option D mischaracterises the charge.

questions.question: Diana plans to poison Eric. She goes to a pharmacy and purchases what she believes is a lethal poison but which is actually a harmless substance. She mixes it into Eric's food. Is Diana guilty of attempted murder?

options.A: No — it is impossible to murder someone with a harmless substance; since completion of the offence was factually impossible, Diana cannot be guilty of attempted murder
options.B: Yes — Diana had the full intention to kill Eric and performed acts proximate to murder (purchasing the substance and mixing it into his food); the fact that the substance was harmless (and the murder therefore factually impossible) does not prevent conviction for attempted murder; applying R v White, impossibility due to factual circumstances does not negate attempt
options.C: Yes — but only if Eric actually consumed the food; if he did not consume it, Diana's acts remain on the preparation side of the line
options.D: No — attempted murder requires a weapon or substance actually capable of causing death; using an inherently harmless substance cannot be proximate to murder

questions.explanation: Diana is guilty of attempted murder. Applying R v White [1910] and the general approach to impossibility in attempt: (1) Diana had the specific intention to kill Eric — the full mens rea for murder; (2) Diana performed acts proximate to murder — she acquired what she believed was poison and mixed it into Eric's food; (3) the fact that the substance was harmless is a factual impossibility that prevented completion of the murder, but does not prevent conviction for attempted murder. The law focuses on the accused's intent and the acts performed, not on the objective capability of those acts to achieve the intended result. The key distinction is between factual impossibility (could not work due to circumstances — not a defence to attempt) and legal impossibility (the thing done is not legally an offence — may be a defence). Purchasing a harmless substance believing it to be poison and administering it is exactly the kind of case where attempt liability appropriately attaches despite impossibility of completion. Options A and D incorrectly apply impossibility as a defence. Option C incorrectly adds a consumption requirement.
